name: Jesus before Pilate
notes: All four Gospels recount the trial of Jesus before Pilate, and their shared core runs in close parallel: Pilate's question "Art thou the King of the Jews?" and Jesus' answer "Thou sayest"; the governor's wonder that turns to a verdict of no fault; the feast-time custom of releasing a prisoner; the choosing of Barabbas; the cry "Crucify him"; Pilate's "Why, what evil hath he done?"; and his final yielding, scourging Jesus and delivering him to be crucified. Each account keeps its own vantage: Matthew alone records Pilate washing his hands and his wife's dream-warning about the just man; Luke alone tells of the sending to Herod and back; and John alone preserves the extended dialogue ("My kingdom is not of this world," "What is truth," "Behold the man," and "Thou couldest have no power at all against me, except it were given thee from above"). These unique portions are complementary windows on the one trial, each evangelist drawing out what the Spirit moved him to record, and they read in place unlinked.
